coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent and electrochemical dual-responsive immunosensor for sensitive and reliable detection of biomarkers based on cation-exchange reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yuting
Cai, Xiaoli
Zhu, Chengzhou
Yang, Haipeng
Du, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and reliable detection of biomarkers is of vital importance in tumor early detection and clinical therapy. A novel fluorescent/electrochemical dual-responsive immunosensing platform for reliable and sensitive quantification of biomarkers was designed based on cation-exchange reaction. To construct such a versatile platform, the model analyte, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), was captured by magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles bound with primary antibodies (Fe3O4-Ab1) and then recognized by the detection antibodies conjugated complex containing poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM), carbon nanotube (CNT) and carboxyl functionalized CdSe nanocrystals (NCs) (CNT-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2). Via ligand exchange, the stable CdSe nanocrystals were easily functionalized with carboxylate ion (CdSe–COO-) and showed high hydrophilicity. The CdSe–COO- was effectively and densely conjugated to CNT coated dendrimer PAMAM that possesses large specific surface area. Finally, the target CEA was detected based on cation-exchange reaction (CER) by adding Ag+ to release thousands of cations Cd2+, which were detected by fluorescence and electrochemistry simultaneously. The electrochemical measurement was performed by directly detecting Cd2+ through square wave voltammetry (SWV), which displayed an excellent correlation with CEA from 5 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.7 pg/mL. The fluorescence detection was implemented since free Cd2+ could trigger the weak fluorescence metal-sensitive dyes (Rhod-5N) to generate extremely high fluorescence signal. The fluorescence results showed the LOD for CEA detection was 0.25 pg/mL with a calibration curve range from 1 pg/mL to 20 ng/mL. The dual signal outputs showed an attractively self-correcting ability, which provides the capability of avoiding false positive signal and making the detection result more reliable. The proposed dual-responsive platform holds great promises for biomarkers detection in clinical diagnostics and therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-responsive immunosensors
Cation-exchange reaction
Biomarkers
CdSe nanocrystals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of capture antibody (Ab1) with Fe3O4 nanoparticles
Dual-responsive assay of the immunosensor after CER
Selectivity and accuracy of the immunosensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Science, Technology and Innovation Commission of Shenzhen Municipality

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CdSe–COOH. The inset is the enlarged TEM image of CdSe–COOH. (B) IR spectra of CdSe NCs before (a) and after (b) ligand exchange and (c) 4-MCBA. TEM image of CNTs (C), CNTs-PAMAM (D) and CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs (E, F).
Fluorescent intensity of the mixture of 100 μL of 1 mM CdSe NCs and 4 μM Rhod-5N after (a) and before (b) cation exchange (CER) reaction with 100 μL of 500 μM Ag+ ions. (λex = 530 nm).

(A) Fluorescent spectra of immunosensor with increasing CEA (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05. 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 20 ng/ml) in the presence of 100 μL of 500 μM AgNO3 and 4 μM Rhod-5N. (λex = 530 nm) (B) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity (at 576 nm) for CEA. (C) SWV curves of immunosensor with increasing CEA (0, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1. 0.5, 1, 5, 10, and 50 ng/ml) in 100 μL of 500 μM AgNO3. (D) Calibration curve of SWV signal (at −0.73 V) for CEA. (SWV was carried out with amplitude of 25 mV, frequency of 25 Hz). Error bars appeared in the figures stand for the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2 complex (A) and the principle of the dual-responsive immunoassay (B).
Comparison of the immunosensing performance towards CEA with other reports.
Results for detection of CEA in human serum by the dual-responsive immunosensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: (these data were the final values after substracting the intrinsic concentration of CEA in the sample).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent and electrochemical dual-responsive immunosensor for sensitive and reliable detection of biomarkers based on cation-exchange reaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and reliable detection of biomarkers is of vital importance in tumor early detection and clinical therapy. A novel fluorescent/electrochemical dual-responsive immunosensing platform for reliable and sensitive quantification of biomarkers was designed based on cation-exchange reaction. To construct such a versatile platform, the model analyte, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), was captured by magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles bound with primary antibodies (Fe3O4-Ab1) and then recognized by the detection antibodies conjugated complex containing poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM), carbon nanotube (CNT) and carboxyl functionalized CdSe nanocrystals (NCs) (CNT-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2). Via ligand exchange, the stable CdSe nanocrystals were easily functionalized with carboxylate ion (CdSe–COO-) and showed high hydrophilicity. The CdSe–COO- was effectively and densely conjugated to CNT coated dendrimer PAMAM that possesses large specific surface area. Finally, the target CEA was detected based on cation-exchange reaction (CER) by adding Ag+ to release thousands of cations Cd2+, which were detected by fluorescence and electrochemistry simultaneously. The electrochemical measurement was performed by directly detecting Cd2+ through square wave voltammetry (SWV), which displayed an excellent correlation with CEA from 5 pg/mL to 50 ng/mL, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 1.7 pg/mL. The fluorescence detection was implemented since free Cd2+ could trigger the weak fluorescence metal-sensitive dyes (Rhod-5N) to generate extremely high fluorescence signal. The fluorescence results showed the LOD for CEA detection was 0.25 pg/mL with a calibration curve range from 1 pg/mL to 20 ng/mL. The dual signal outputs showed an attractively self-correcting ability, which provides the capability of avoiding false positive signal and making the detection result more reliable. The proposed dual-responsive platform holds great promises for biomarkers detection in clinical diagnostics and therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Globally, cancer is one of the leading causes of mortality and nearly 1 in 6 deaths is due to cancer. Despite cancer research has received considerable attention and funding, the 5-year relative survival rates of sufferers who are diagnosed with advanced cancer are still extremely low (e.g. 3% for lung cancer). Fortunately, researches reveal that the survival rate can be significantly improved when cancers are discovered at an early stage (50% for lung cancer) [1]. Therefore, early detection of cancer is of great significance to reduction mortality. One of the most important methods for early detection is to measure available blood tumor markers (biomarkers). These biomarkers are generated by either tumor itself or human body in response to the tumor growth, thus they could be good indicators for the presence, growth, and metastasis of tumors. Generally, various immunoassays have been reported to assay biomarkers, including colorimetric immunoassay [2,3], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [4], chemiluminescence immunoassay [5–7], surface plasmon resonance immunoassay [8,9], electrochemical immunoassay [10–14], electrochemiluminescent immunoassay [15] and fluorescence immunoassay [16,17]. In comparison to other counterparts, electrochemical immunoassay (EIA) has captured lots of attention due to its low-cost, high sensitivity and miniaturization, thus showing promise in clinical application [18]. However, the major drawback of EIA is that they lack enough stability, which limits its successful clinical applications.

Dual-responsive assay strategies can solve this problem, because two types of analytical platforms can offer two types of signal outputs simultaneously to ensure the accuracy and persuasiveness of the detection results [19]. Up to now, many dual-responsive assay platforms have been developed, such as fluorescent-colorimetric [20,21], Surface enhanced Raman scattering-fluorescent [22], magnetic-fluorescent [23] and electrochemical-fluorescent [24]. Among them, electrochemical-fluorescent (EC-FL) dual-responsive assay has gained much attention because this method could provide sensitive electrochemical signal and stable fluorescence response simultaneously. For example, Lapos et al. have reported an EC-FL dual-responsive detection of various analytes on electrophoresis microchip [24]. Kevin et al. assembled copper nanoblocks capable of sensitively detecting multiple RNA samples with flexible FL and EC readouts. However, thus far, EC-FL dual-responsive assay for immunoassay biomarkers has not been reported.

In this study, we developed a sensitive EC-FL dual-responsive immunoassay based on cation exchange reaction (CER) for the early detection of biomarkers. CER was first reported by Alivisatos and co-workers [25], which is a gentle, fast response and high efficiency method to generate divalent cations. CdSe was adopted in the current study because its CER mechanism has been demonstrated [25] and a large amount of synthesis methods and various surface modification strategies have been developed, which facilitates their applications in bioassays [26–28]. The CER equation was shown in Fig. S1, in which Ag+ exchanged CdSe NCs to Ag2Se and a large number of Cd2+. The formation of Ag2Se is due to the thermodynamic driving force, the Ag2Se is more thermodynamically favored compared with CdSe [29]. If we combine CER with metal-sensitive dyes, thousands of Cd2+ can be released from each NC and the Cd2+ can trigger the non- or weakly fluorescence dye molecule to obtain hundreds of folds increase in FL signal intensity [30]. Such a CER system is a simple and highly sensitive method, which could be developed for immunoassay biomarkers [31,32].

Herein, several kinds of nanomaterials were combined in constructing the immunosensor. Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) are widely used in immunosensors since the magnetic NPs can provide an easy way to separate the immunocomplex. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) play a leading role in nanomedicine and biosensing due to their large specific surface area. The water-soluble carboxylate ion functionalized CdSe NCs were obtained by ligand exchange. Poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) dendrimers exhibited a high density of functional groups, excellent solubility and high branched units, making them perfect candidates for linking CNTs, CdSe NCs and recognition antibody. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was selected as the model biomarker which is widely used to evaluate many kinds of malignancies, such as lung cancer, colorectal cancer and ovarian cancer. The immunosensor was constructed as follows (Scheme 1 ): CEA was firstly captured by magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles bound primary antibodies (Fe3O4-Ab1) and then recognized by the detection antibodies conjugated compound containing PAMAM, CNTs and carboxylate ion functionalized CdSe nanocrystals (CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2). Finally, the target CEA was detected by adding Ag+ to release thousands of Cd2+, which were detected by fluorescence and electrochemistry. The nanomaterials (PAMAM coated CNTs) combining CER could extremely amplify the detection signal, and the fluorescence and electrochemistry dual-response could guarantee a more convincing result. Therefore, the proposed immunosensor exhibited promising future in early detection of various biomarkers in clinical diagnostics and therapy.

SeO2, cadmium myristate, 1-octadecene, oleic acid, 4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MCBA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), NaOH, AgNO3, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Alpha-Fetoprotein (AFP), Immunoglobulin G (IgG) and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, United States). Carboxylic Multi-Walled Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) was purchased from XFNANO (Nanjing, China). Poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) (generation 4.0) was obtained from Weihai CY Dendrimer Technology Co., Ltd. (Shangdong, China). Carboxylated Fe3O4 (Fe3O4–COOH) was obtained from Tianjin Baseline ChromTech Research Centre (Tianjin, China). Carcino Embryonic Antigen capture antibody (Ab1), Carcino Embryonic Antigen detection antibody (Ab2) and Carcino Embryonic Antigen (CEA) were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai, China).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a FluoroMax-P spectrophotometer (HORIBA, JOBIN YVON). The electrochemical measurements were carried out on a standard three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI 660). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired from a Tecnai G2 F30 field emission gun transmission electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were recorded using a JEOL-2100F electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance powder X-ray diffraction system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Physical Electronics Quantera scanning X-ray microprobe. Quartz crystal microbalance QCM 200 (Stanford Research Systems, USA) was used to characterize the specific recognition of antigen and antibody.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of capture antibody (Ab1) with Fe3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugation of Ab1 and Fe3O4 was prepared through an amide binding between the carboxy-terminated Fe3O4 and Ab1 with an EDC/NHS amidization reaction. Firstly, 1.0 mL of 1.0 mg/mL Fe3O4–COOH were washed twice with 1 mL of 25 mM MES (pH 6). Then, 150 μL of 50 mg/mL EDC and 150 μL of 50 mg/mL NHS were added to the Fe3O4 NPs solution. After incubation for 30 min, the mixture was separated by a magnet. Then, 50 μL of 1.0 mg/mL Ab1 and 950 μL of 25 mM MES were added to the Fe3O4 NPs and stirred gently overnight. The obtained Fe3O4 NPs-Ab1 complex was washed twice and stored in 1.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) with 1.0% BSA at 4 °C.

The stable CdSe NCs were synthesized without degassing according to Cao et al. [33]. Briefly, 0.1 mmol SeO2, 0.1 mmol cadmium myristate and 6.3 mL 1-octadecene were added to a three-neck flask. The compound was heated to 240 °C and maintained for 15 min. Then, 0.1 mL oleic acid was added into the solution at the rate of one drop per 10 s. Subsequently, the reaction temperature was maintained for 30 min, and then cooled down to room temperature. The resultant CdSe NCs were precipitated with acetone, and re-dispersed in 4 mL tetrahydrofuran (THF).

The carboxyl-functionalized CdSe NCs were obtained based on previously reported method [34]. Firstly, 40 mg 4-mercaptobenzoic acid and 5 mL THF were added to a three-neck flask. After heating the solution to 50 °C, 2 mL as-synthesized CdSe NCs were added and incubated for 5 h. Then, CdSe–COOH was precipitated by adding 200 μL of 0.5 M NaOH, washed with deionized water and stored at 4 °C. By this step, the surface of CdSe NCs convert to carboxylate ions (CdSe–COO-).

The carboxylic CNTs were first activated by 50 mg/mL EDC and NHS for 30 min. The activated CNTs were purified by centrifugation and washed with deionized water. And then, 0.2 mL PAMAM aqueous solution was added and reacted for 2 h under shaking. The obtained CNTs-PAMAM was purified and washed by centrifugation. Subsequently, 1.0 mL CdSe–COO- and 50 μL of 1.0 mg/mL Ab2 were added to the complex and stirred for 5 h, the obtained CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2 was washed twice and stored in 1.0 mL of PBS (pH 7.4) with 1% BSA at 4 °C. Here, some of the Ab2 were conjugated with CdSe based on the amide binding. Other Ab2 were conjugated with PAMAM via the electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions between the PAMAM and amino acid residues in Ab2 [35].

100 μL of CEA antigen with different desired concentrations were mixed with 50 μL of Fe3O4-Ab1 in each tube for 40 min under gentle shaking. After the supernatant was removed by magnetic separation, the Fe3O4-Ab1-CEA was washed twice with PBST (PBS containing 0.05% Tween-20). Afterwards, 50 μL CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs-Ab2 immunocomplex was added and stirred for another 40 min. Subsequently, the Fe3O4-Ab1-CEA-Ab2-CdSe NCs-PAMAM-CNTs complex was washed with PBST and re-dispersed in 100 μL PBS. After addition 100 μL of 500 μM AgNO3, Cd2+ was released to the solution. Then, the solution was magnetically separated, and the supernatant was divided into two parts for the following detection. The created relationship between the concentration of CEA and the amount of Cd2+ was applied to assay the desired samples. One part of the supernatant was measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer. After adding 4 μM Rhod-5N to the target solution, the fluorescence was recorded under the excitation wavelength of 530 nm.

The other part of the supernatant was investigated by the electrochemical method. A three-electrode system was adopted with glassy carbon electrode (GCE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum wire electrode. Before electrochemical detection, the GCE was polished with alumina and sonicated in ethanol and distilled water until the peaks separation of the cyclic voltammeter test in K4[Fe(CN)6]/K3[Fe(CN)6] solution lower than 80 mV. First, the three electrodes were immersed into the acetate buffer (pH 5.0) that contained the target supernatant. Then, Cd2+ was deposited at the potential of −1.1 V for 300 s, and stripped by using square wave voltammetry (SWV) at the potential range from −1.0 to −0.2 V.

TEM image (Fig. 1 A) revealed that the CdSe NCs possessed a uniform diameter of 4.5 nm. Infrared spectra (IR) (Fig. 1B) shows that the strong absorption around 2910 cm−1 (C–H stretching peaks from 1-octadecene) disappeared after carboxylation with 4-MCBA, indicating the ligand exchange was accomplished. The photographs in Fig. S2 displayed that the CdSe NCs were transferred to the aqueous solution from organic phase after functionalization. All the above results demonstrate that the hydrophilic and carboxyl functionalized CdSe NCs were successfully prepared.

The changes of morphologies during the synthesis of CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs were observed by TEM. Compared with the pure CNTs (Fig. 1C), a uniformly indistinct thin layer of 4–5 nm thickness was observed on the surface of CNTs-PAMAM (Fig. 1D), indicating the PAMAM was successfully covalently bonded to CNTs. As seen in Fig. 1E and F, it was evident that the surface of CNTs-PAMAM was well-distributed with CdSe NCs and the CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs hybrid materials were stable since nearly no free CdSe NCs were detected after 30 days. As support, IR spectroscopy (Fig. S3A) and Zeta potential (Fig. S3B) was further used to characterize the CdSe NCs and Ab2 were successfully coupled to CNTs-PAMAM. For CNTs-COOH, the band at 1713 cm-1 corresponded to CO. For PAMAM, the absorption peak occurred at 1654 and 1555 cm-1, which can be assigned to amide (–CO–NH-) I and II. The absorption peak at 1713 cm-1 almost disappeared in CNTs-PAMAM, demonstrating the reaction of carboxyl groups in CNTs with PAMAM. The other part of the curve of CNTs-PAMAM was similar to that of PAMAM, indicating PAMAM dendrimers were coupled to CNTs [36]. In addition, the CO stretch vibration at 1707 cm−1 in CdSe–COOH weakened and blue shifted to 1687 cm−1 in CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs, demonstrating the formation of an amide. These results further proved that CdSe NCs were successfully coupled to CNTs-PAMAM. As depicted in Fig. S2B, the intrinsic zeta potential of CNTs-COOH was −14 mV. After the conjugation with PAMAM, the surface charge greatly increased to +34 mV. Then the value of zeta potential of CNTs-PAMAM-CdSe NCs changed to −15 mV and slightly increased after the conjugation of Ab2.

The dual-responsive immunoassay for the detection of CEA was fabricated using Fe3O4 NPs for the conjugation of Fe3O4 NPs-functionalized Ab1, since the magnetic Fe3O4 NPs offered an easy way to construct the immunocomplex. As shown in Fig. S4A, after adding an external magnet, the immunocomplex was easily separated due to the magnetism of the compound. The SEM image in Fig. S4B shows the Fe3O4 NPs were relatively monodispersed with an average diameter of 130 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-responsive assay of the immunosensor after CER
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental conditions of the immune-recognition time, the CER incubation time, and the concentrations of Rhod-5N and Ag+ were optimized as shown in Fig. S5. To demonstrate the excellent signal amplification capability of the immunosensor, the fluorescence intensity of the CdSe NCs before and after CER was investigated. The original fluorescence of the carboxylate CdSe NCs nearly disappeared during the ligand exchange with 4-MCBA due to the introduction of surface defect sites [28]. As shown in Fig. 2 , the solution of CdSe NCs and Rhod-5N displays negligible fluorescence, but the fluorescence intensity is significantly enhanced after the CER with Ag+ (10-fold enhancement), indicating the CER could serve as a sensitive detection method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and accuracy of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the dual signal response of the immunosensor, other possible interferences including BSA, AFP and IgG were used in this study. Fluorescent signals and current responses of the prepared immunosensor in 5 ng/mL CEA, BSA, AFP or 5 ng/mL CEA containing the same concentration of IgG or BSA were measured (Fig. S7B). The results reveal that the immunosensor did not show any significant responses for other interferences, indicating the excellent selectivity of the proposed dual-responsive immunosensor.

To evaluate the accuracy and the potential application of the dual-responsive immunosensor, a recovery test was conducted in human serum. For each sample, the measurement was repeated three times. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery is between 95% and 106% with RSD values ranging from 3.2% to 8.6% for the two methods, implying the acceptable accuracy and potential application of the developed immunosensor for the detection of CEA in real samples.

In summary, an electrochemistry/fluorescence dual-responsive sandwich immunosensor was developed based on CER to detect CEA. The dual signal outputs showed an attractive self-correcting ability, which is capable of avoiding false positive signal and making the detection result extremely accurate. The sensitivity of the immunosensor was also greatly enhanced due to the CER could release thousands of Cd2+, which could obtain 10-fold signal amplification. The amplification label Ab2-CdSe NCs-PAMAM-CNTs could further enhance the signal because the large specific surface area of the CNTs and PAMAM is capable of binding more signal tags. Therefore, the sensitive and accurate immunosensor hold great promises in tumor early detection, clinical therapy and global health screening. In the future, the sensitivity of the proposed methods can to be further improved by replacing CdSe NCs to other new NCs with superior performance.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors./All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Committee (Grant No. JCYJ20180305124343508) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2018M643145).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.10.054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Early detection of cancer: immunoassays for plasma tumor markers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. L. Meany, L. J. Sokoll, D. W. Chan, Early detection of cancer: immunoassays for plasma tumor markers, Expert Opin. Med. Diagn. 3 (2009) 597-605.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoresponsive colorimetric immunoassay based on chitosan modified AgI/TiO2 heterojunction for highly sensitive chloramphenicol detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chang, J. Lv, H. Zhang, B. Zhang, W. Wei, Y. Qiao, Photoresponsive colorimetric immunoassay based on chitosan modified AgI/TiO2 heterojunction for highly sensitive chloramphenicol detection, Biosens. Bioelectron. 87 (2017) 579-586.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A lateral flow assay for the determination of human tetanus antibody in whole blood by using gold nanoparticle labeled tetanus antigen

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prospective studies on the routine use of a novel multivariant enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the diagnosis of autoimmune bullous diseases

Geller, Prospective studies on the routine use of a novel multivariant enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for the diagnosis of autoimmune bullous diseases, J. Am. Acad. Dermatol. 76 (2017) 889-894.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The chemiluminescence immunoassay for aflatoxin B1 based on functionalized magnetic nanoparticles with two strategies of antigen probe immobilization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yao, L. Wang, C. Fang, The chemiluminescence immunoassay for aflatoxin B1 based on functionalized magnetic nanoparticles with two strategies of antigen probe immobilization, Luminescence 32 (2017) 661-665.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence reaction kinetics-resolved multianalyte immunoassay strategy using a bispecific monoclonal antibody as the unique recognition reagent

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultra-facile and label-free immunoassay strategy for detection of copper (II) utilizing chemiluminescence self-enhancement of Cu (II)-ethylenediaminetetraacetate chelate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ouyang, Q. Shu, W. Wang, Z. Wang, S. Yang, L. Wang, Z. Fu, An ultra-facile and label-free immunoassay strategy for detection of copper (II) utilizing chemiluminescence self-enhancement of Cu (II)-ethylenediaminetetraacetate chelate, Biosens. Bioelectron. 85 (2016) 157-163.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticle-enhanced multiplexed imaging surface plasmon resonance (iSPR) detection of Fusarium mycotoxins in wheat

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Z. Hossain, C. M. Maragos, Gold nanoparticle-enhanced multiplexed imaging surface plasmon resonance (iSPR) detection of Fusarium mycotoxins in wheat, Biosens. Bioelectron. 101 (2018) 245-252.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid localized surface plasmon resonance and quartz crystal microbalance sensor for label free biosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Hao, C. Hu, J. Grant, A. Glidle, D. R. Cumming, Hybrid localized surface plasmon resonance and quartz crystal microbalance sensor for label free biosensing, Biosens. Bioelectron. 100 (2018) 23-27.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of an electrochemical immunosensor for the diagnostic testing of spotted fever using synthetic peptides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. C. Prado, M. E. Chino, A. L. dos Santos, A. L. Souza, L. G. Pinho, E. R. Lemos, S. G. De-Simone, Development of an electrochemical immunosensor for the diagnostic testing of spotted fever using synthetic peptides, Biosens. Bioelectron. 100 (2018) 115-121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glucose encapsulating liposome for signal amplification for quantitative detection of biomarkers with glucometer readout

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao, D. Du, Y. Lin, Glucose encapsulating liposome for signal amplification for quantitative detection of biomarkers with glucometer readout, Biosens. Bioelectron. 72 (2015) 348-354.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrated immunochromatographic strip with glucometer readout for rapid quantification of phosphorylated proteins
